sourceQuestion:
How many distinct BSTs can be constructed with 3 distinct keys?

A. 4
B. 5
C. 6
D. 9

parsed.stem: How many distinct BSTs can be constructed with 3 distinct keys?

sourceQuestion:
Which one of the following statement(s) is/are FALSE?

A. Overlaying is used to run a program, which is longer than the address space of the computer.
B. Optimal binary search tree construction can be performed efficiently by using dynamic programming.
C. Depth first search cannot be used to find connected components of a graph.
D. Given the prefix and postfix walks over a binary tree, the binary tree can be uniquely constructed.

parsed.stem: Which one of the following statement(s) is/are FALSE?

options.text: Overlaying is used to run a program, which is longer than the address space of the computer.
Optimal binary search tree construction can be performed efficiently by using dynamic programming.
Depth first search cannot be used to find connected components of a graph.
Given the prefix and postfix walks over a binary tree, the binary tree can be uniquely constructed.

sourceQuestion:
The total external path length, EPL, of a binary tree with n external nodes is sum_{i=1}^{n} l_i, where l_i is the path length of external node i. Which of the following is/are TRUE?

A. EPL is always >= n*log_2(n).
B. EPL >= n*log_2(n) always.
C. EPL is equal to IPL + 2n always.
D. EPL = O(n*log_2(n)) for some special trees.

parsed.stem: The total external path length, EPL, of a binary tree with n external nodes is sum_{i=1}^{n} l_i, where l_i is the path length of external node i. Which of the following is/are TRUE?

options.text: EPL is equal to IPL + 2n always.
EPL = O(n*log_2(n)) for some special trees.

sourceQuestion:
If the binary tree in the figure is traversed in inorder, then the order in which the nodes will be visited is ______.

The binary tree has the following structure:
- Root: 7
  - Left child of 7: 3
    - Left child of 3: 1
    - Right child of 3: 4
  - Right child of 7: 9
    - Left child of 9: 8
    - Right child of 9: (none)

A. 1 3 4 7 8 9
B. 1 4 3 8 9 7
C. 7 3 1 4 9 8
D. 1 3 4 7 9 8

parsed.stem:
If the binary tree in the figure is traversed in inorder, then the order in which the nodes will be visited is ______.

The binary tree has the following structure:
- Root: 7
  - Left child of 7: 3
    - Left child of 3: 1
    - Right child of 3: 4
  - Right child of 7: 9
    - Left child of 9: 8
    - Right child of 9: (none)

sourceQuestion:
A binary tree T has n leaf nodes. The number of nodes of degree 2 in T is:
A. log_2(n)
B. n - 1
C. n
D. 2^n

sourceQuestion:
Which of the following sequences denotes the post-order traversal sequence of the tree shown below?

The tree structure (from image):
- Root: a
- Left child of a: b
- Right child of a: e
- Left child of b: c
- Right child of b: d
- Left child of e: f
- Right child of e: g

A. f, e, g, c, b, a
B. g, c, b, d, a, f, e
C. g, c, b, f, e, a
D. f, c, b, g, e, d, a
sourceAnswer: D. f, c, b, g, e, d, a

parsed.stem:
Which of the following sequences denotes the post-order traversal sequence of the tree shown below?

The tree structure (from image):
- Root: a
- Left child of a: b
- Right child of a: e
- Left child of b: c
- Right child of b: d
- Left child of e: f
- Right child of e: g

options.text: f, e, g, c, b, a
g, c, b, d, a, f, e
g, c, b, f, e, a
f, c, b, g, e, d, a

sourceQuestion:
Consider the following nested representation of binary trees: (X Y Z) indicates a tree with X as the root, Y as the left subtree and Z as the right subtree. Note that Y and Z may be NULL or further nested. Which of the following represents a valid binary tree?
A. (1 (2 3 4) (5 6 7))
B. (1 (2 3 4) (5 6 7 8))
C. (1 (2 3 NULL) (5 6 7))
D. (1 (2 3 4) NULL)

parsed.stem: Consider the following nested representation of binary trees: (X Y Z) indicates a tree with X as the root, Y as the left subtree and Z as the right subtree. Note that Y and Z may be NULL or further nested. Which of the following represents a valid binary tree?

sourceQuestion:
Let LASTPOST, LASTIN and LASTPRE denote the last vertex visited in a postorder, inorder and preorder traversal respectively, of a complete binary tree. Which of the following is always true?
A. LASTIN = LASTPOST
B. LASTIN = LASTPRE
C. LASTPRE = LASTPOST
D. None of the above

parsed.stem: Let LASTPOST, LASTIN and LASTPRE denote the last vertex visited in a postorder, inorder and preorder traversal respectively, of a complete binary tree. Which of the following is always true?

sourceQuestion:
A weight-balanced tree is a binary tree in which for each node, the number of nodes in the left sub tree is at least half and at most twice the number of nodes in the right sub tree. The maximum possible height (number of nodes on the path from the root to the furthest leaf) of such a tree on n nodes is best described by which of the following?
A. log_2(n)
B. log_(4/3)(n)
C. log_3(n)
D. log_(3/2)(n)

parsed.stem: A weight-balanced tree is a binary tree in which for each node, the number of nodes in the left sub tree is at least half and at most twice the number of nodes in the right sub tree. The maximum possible height (number of nodes on the path from the root to the furthest leaf) of such a tree on n nodes is best described by which of the following?

sourceQuestion:
Consider the label sequences obtained by the following pairs of traversals on a labeled binary tree. Which of these pairs identify a tree uniquely?
I. preorder and postorder
II. inorder and postorder
III. preorder and inorder
IV. level order and postorder
A. I only
B. II, III
C. III only
D. IV only

parsed.stem:
Consider the label sequences obtained by the following pairs of traversals on a labeled binary tree. Which of these pairs identify a tree uniquely?
I. preorder and postorder
II. inorder and postorder
III. preorder and inorder
IV. level order and postorder

sourceAnswer: A. The number of leaf nodes in the tree

options.text: The number of leaf nodes in the tree
The number of nodes in the tree
The number of internal nodes in the tree

sourceQuestion:
A scheme for storing binary trees in an array X is as follows. Indexing of X starts at 1 instead of 0. The root is stored at X[1]. For a node stored at X[i], the left child, if any, is stored in X[2i] and the right child, if any, is stored in X[2i+1]. To be able to store any binary tree on n vertices, the minimum size of X should be:

A. log_2(n)
B. n
C. 2n + 1
D. 2^n - 1

parsed.stem: A scheme for storing binary trees in an array X is as follows. Indexing of X starts at 1 instead of 0. The root is stored at X[1]. For a node stored at X[i], the left child, if any, is stored in X[2i] and the right child, if any, is stored in X[2i+1]. To be able to store any binary tree on n vertices, the minimum size of X should be:

sourceQuestion:
The height of a binary tree is the maximum number of edges in any root to leaf path. The maximum number of nodes in a binary tree of height h is:

A. 2^h - 1
B. 2^(h-1) - 1
C. 2^(h+1) - 1
D. 2^(h+1)

parsed.stem: The height of a binary tree is the maximum number of edges in any root to leaf path. The maximum number of nodes in a binary tree of height h is:

sourceQuestion:
The inorder and preorder traversal of a binary tree are d b e a f c g a and a b d e f c g, respectively. The postorder traversal of the binary tree is:
A. d e b f g c a
B. e d b g f c a
C. e d b f g c a
D. d e f g b c a
sourceAnswer: C. e d b f g c a

parsed.stem: The inorder and preorder traversal of a binary tree are d b e a f c g a and a b d e f c g, respectively. The postorder traversal of the binary tree is:

options.text: d e b f g c a
e d b g f c a
e d b f g c a
d e f g b c a

sourceAnswer: A. the number of leaf nodes in the tree

options.text: the number of leaf nodes in the tree
the number of internal nodes in the tree
the number of leaf nodes in the tree

sourceQuestion:
In a binary tree with n nodes, every node has an odd number of descendants. Every node is considered to be its own descendant. What is the number of nodes in the tree that have exactly one child?
A. 0
B. 1
C. (n-1)/2
D. n-1

parsed.stem: In a binary tree with n nodes, every node has an odd number of descendants. Every node is considered to be its own descendant. What is the number of nodes in the tree that have exactly one child?

sourceQuestion:
We are given a set of n distinct elements and an unlabeled binary tree with n nodes. In how many ways can we populate the tree with the given set so that it becomes a binary search tree?
A. 0
B. 1
C. n!
D. (1/n) * C(2n, n)

parsed.stem: We are given a set of n distinct elements and an unlabeled binary tree with n nodes. In how many ways can we populate the tree with the given set so that it becomes a binary search tree?

D. 31 and 5, respectively

sourceQuestion:
Consider a binary tree T in which every node has either zero or two children. Let n > 0 be the number of nodes in T. Which ONE of the following is the number of nodes in T that have exactly two children?

A. (n-1)/2
B. n/2
C. n/4
D. (n+1)/2

parsed.stem: Consider a binary tree T in which every node has either zero or two children. Let n > 0 be the number of nodes in T. Which ONE of the following is the number of nodes in T that have exactly two children?

sourceQuestion:
Consider the following tree traversals on a full binary tree:
i. Preorder
ii. Inorder
iii. Postorder

Which of the following traversal options is/are sufficient to uniquely reconstruct the full binary tree?

A. (i) and (ii)
B. (ii) and (iii)
C. (i) and (iii)
D. (ii) only

i. Preorder

sourceQuestion:
Let H, I, L, and N represent height, number of internal nodes, number of leaf nodes, and the total number of nodes respectively in a rooted binary tree. Which of the following statements is/are always TRUE?

A. L <= I + 1
B. H + 1 <= N <= 2^(H+1) - 1
C. H <= I <= 2^(H-1)
D. H <= L <= 2^(H-1)

parsed.stem: Let H, I, L, and N represent height, number of internal nodes, number of leaf nodes, and the total number of nodes respectively in a rooted binary tree. Which of the following statements is/are always TRUE?

sourceQuestion:
The following three are known to be the preorder, inorder and postorder sequences of a binary tree. But it is not known which is which.
I.   MBDCAFHPYK
II.  KAMCBDYFPH
III. MABDCYFPH
Pick the true statement from the following.
A. I and II are the preorder and inorder sequences, respectively
B. I and II are the inorder and postorder sequences, respectively
C. II is the inorder sequence, but nothing more can be said about the other two sequences
D. II and III are the preorder and inorder sequences, respectively
sourceAnswer: B. I and II are the inorder and postorder sequences, respectively

parsed.stem:
The following three are known to be the preorder, inorder and postorder sequences of a binary tree. But it is not known which is which.
I.   MBDCAFHPYK
II.  KAMCBDYFPH
III. MABDCYFPH
Pick the true statement from the following.

options.text: I and II are the preorder and inorder sequences, respectively
I and II are the inorder and postorder sequences, respectively
II is the inorder sequence, but nothing more can be said about the other two sequences
II and III are the preorder and inorder sequences, respectively
